text: During the robotic prostatectomy, the surgeon performs a modified apical dissection by carefully removing fat from the apex. Superficial dorsal veins encountered are cauterized to maintain hemostasis.
The surgeon exposes the endopelvic fascia, revealing the location of the puboprostatic ligaments. The detached fat pad is then removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia, separating the prostate from the pelvic side wall. The left endopelvic fascia is also incised, revealing a superficial vein running along the prostate capsule. The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to achieve hemostasis on this rare anatomic anomaly.

The surgeon identifies the tabernus nerve running posterior and lateral to the prostate, ensuring it remains unharmed during the procedure. A perforating vessel located between the pelvic sidewall and the prostate, lateral to the puboprostatic ligament, is controlled with a bipolar instrument before division.

The surgeon places a stitch over the apex of the prostate to control the dorsal vein complex, carefully avoiding penetration into the prostate tissue itself. Instruments are used to squeeze and isolate the dorsal vein complex, which contains blood vessels requiring control.
The surgeon uses the left-sided instrument to squeeze the prostate away, isolating the dorsal vein structures as the apical structure is divided following the majority of the prostatectomy.
The surgeon uses cautery between the left hand and the previously placed stitch, ensuring not to cut the stitch to prevent significant bleeding and avoiding violation of the prostate tissue.

The surgeon isolates the urethra and divides it to expose the Foley catheter, ensuring the dissection is just beyond the prostate apex while preserving the maximum length of urethral tissue. The Foley catheter is pulled back, leaving only its tip visible.

The surgeon carefully performs posterior urethral dissection, following the contour of the urethral stump to avoid damaging the prostate apex. After detachment, the prostate is placed into an endocatch bag for removal.
